kurzinfo: Microplastics are ubiquitous in various bodies of water. It is estimated that 4.8-12.7 million tonnes of plastic entered the oceans in 2010. Plastic pollution is classified as an emerging risk and poses a global threat to marine ecosystems. Various studies (and subsequent media reports) report contamination of microplastics in table salt and drinking water. The amounts vary, but there is agreement that microplastics are found in table salt in different countries. Inorganic and organic particles can adhere to microplastics, which are then absorbed by the human body through consumption of contaminated salt. In addition, various types of plastic contain plasticisers, which can have a negative impact on health.

titel: Innovative cocoa substitutes and changes in the manufacturing process of cocoa products due to rising cocoa prices
kurzinfo: Because of rising cocoa prices during the last years, manufacturers are exploring cocoa alternatives, such as microencapsulated aromas, cocoa shell extracts and plant-based fat mimetics. The risk of mislabelling or misrepresentation might increase accordingly. Even when coca prices drop at the moment the problem may remain.   Examples for adjustments might be:  Fetriyuna, et al. "Cocoa Bean Shells: A Potential Chocolate Replacement in Food Production." International Journal on Advanced Science, Engineering & Information Technology 15.1 (2025): “The cocoa bean shells (CBS), a by-product of the cocoa processing industry, are gaining attention for their rich polyphenolic composition, high fiber content, and promising nutraceutical qualities, making them an excellent clean-label ingredient for a variety of food applications. CBS offers a unique scent of cocoa and chocolate, providing a novel and affordable way to flavor or even replace cocoa in a variety of products.”   Writtika Das, Sweta Das, Shairee Ganguly, Dolanchapa Sikdar and Kakali Bandyopadhyay, “A Review on Cocoa Butter Alternatives in Chocolate Prep “Cocoa butter makes up about 50% of cocoa nibs and is highly oxidation-resistant due to natural tocopherols. It is brittle at room temperature and melts at 34–38 °C. However, it is expensive and subject to price fluctuations, so alternatives are used: cocoa-butter equivalents, substitutes, and replacers, often based on vegetable oils such as coconut, palm, soybean, and rapeseed oil. These alternatives can improve fat stability, reduce fat migration, increase softness, and simplify processing because they do not require tempering, while still enabling chocolate production with desirable texture.”
The microbiological safety of insects approved for consumption is the subject of various studies. These products are relatively new on the market and corresponding experience is lacking.

Streptococcus agalactiae is a gram-positive bacterium that can cause various infectious diseases. Previously. the pathogen was mainly known to cause severe neonatal infections (e.g., neonatal sepsis), but now S. agalactiae has also been linked to food, specifically the consumption of raw fish. In 2015, there was a large outbreak of severe invasive infections in Singapore, predominantly septic arthritis and meningitis, which could be traced back to sequence type (ST)283 of S. agalactiae. Epidemiological investigations revealed that the consumption of raw freshwater fish (tilapia) was the trigger. Follow-up investigations used whole genome sequencing (WGS) to assess the prevalence of the bacterium more precisely. The result: S. agalactiae ST283 seems to have caused diseases in humans and farmed fish in Southeast Asia for decades. However, much remains unclear. If ST283 is transmitted exclusively from fish to humans, as was the case in the Singapore outbreak, then cases of S. agalactiae sepsis in Thailand and Laos are predominantly a food-borne disease. However, whether the transmission is from aquaculture to humans or vice versa, and whether there is an as yet unidentified reservoir, remains unknown. The germ could also be detected in tilapia from Brazil. Until now, the serotypes ST260 and ST927 were known in Brazil, but now ST283 also seems to be widespread. Based on import data of live tilapia from Singapore in 2014, a study suspects that the genotype was introduced into the country by the imported fish.

Bacteria of the genus Acinetobacter have become a major problem in hospitals around the world because they often develop resistances to antimicrobial drugs and disinfectants. A new study has now been able to find evidence of the same bacteria in food and food-producing animals. The study collected and analysed a wide variety of chicken, pork and beef meat samples from markets in Lima and Peru. Researchers found 12 Acinetobacter isolates from five different beef samples sold at two unrelated markets. However, it has to be mentioned that the Acinetobacter isolates found in this study had a low prevalence and were not multi-resistant. Raw meat thus appears to be a new potential source of infection for humans or a reservoir for Acinetobacter bacteria respectively.

Since 1980, scientific literature has reported on the links between the prevalence of certain health conditions and the high consumption of ultra-processed food (see NOVA Klassifikation for definition of ultraprocessed food). The increased consumption of this type of food may be responsible for the increase in incidence of many health conditions such as obesity, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, loss of microbial diversity, dysbiosis, other inflammation-related diseases of the digestive system, increased risk of ischaemic heart disease, coronary heart disease and cancer. Ultra-processed foods have high energy density (energy per volume) and low nutrient density (nutrient per unit energy), making them nutritionally unbalanced. Additives are often used to improve shelf life and taste, and chemical contaminants can result from processing methods. Maximum permitted levels for food additives and contaminants are set to protect consumers, but their cumulative effects are largely unknown.

titel: Climate change and its impact on food safety
kurzinfo: Climate change is likely to have a considerable impact on food safety, both direct and indirect, putting public health at risk. With changing rainfall patterns and increases in extreme weather conditions and the annual average temperature, we will begin to face the impacts of climate change. These impacts will affect the persistence and occurrence of bacteria, viruses, parasites, harmful algae, fungi and their vectors, and the patterns of their corresponding food-borne diseases and risk of toxic contamination. Alongside these impacts, chemical residues of pesticides and veterinary medicines in plant and animal products will be affected by changes in pest pressure. The risk of food contamination with heavy metals and persistent organic pollutants following changes in crop varieties cultivated, cultivation methods, soils, redistribution of sediments and long-range atmospheric transport, is increased because of climate change. Climate-sensitive risk factors and illnesses will be among the largest contributors to the global burden of food-related disease and mortality, including malnutrition, communicable, non-communicable, and diarrheal- and vector-borne diseases.
